high_consensus.description: High-impact security and operations gap with low documentation cost. Secret location, rotation, and relationship to the keypair model are essential for a reliable implementation and incident response.
Core product logic is underspecified. A deterministic answer-to-archetype algorithm is required for correctness, consistency across deployments, testing, and downstream integrations.

Valuable freshness rule that closes a real semantic and replay-adjacent gap. Slightly less critical than signing/canonicalization issues, but cheap to specify and likely important to the product narrative.

Failure-mode policy for nonce storage is important because either fail-open or fail-closed has meaningful security and availability consequences. This should be decided explicitly before implementation.

Canonical encoding must be byte-identical across TypeScript and Rust. Ambiguity here can cause hard-to-debug mint failures and security validation inconsistencies, while the fix is mostly specification work.
Important UX and idempotency behavior gap. The on-chain uniqueness rule is clear, but the API and user-facing semantics need to be explicit to avoid confusing repeated submissions.
The ambient stats are central to the stated awareness-layer demonstration, and missing fixture schema plus aggregation rules would block reliable implementation and rehearsal.

disputed.description: Useful fallback specification. It prevents demo-pressure ambiguity if the mint route degrades to witness-only behavior, though it is more UX/contingency planning than core protocol correctness.
Strong operational security issue. Rate-limit identity behind Vercel/CDN is easy to get wrong and should be specified, though it is less central than signature and mint correctness.
Concrete validation bug class with a cheap test addition. Enforcing the step-to-answer-count invariant is important because HMAC integrity alone does not imply state validity.

blockers.concern: Canonical serialization is not specified across TypeScript, Rust, HMAC, quiz hashes, and ed25519 ClaimMessage bytes.
blockers.why_matters: A single mismatch in field order, integer encoding, string normalization, date encoding, CBOR implementation, or Rust struct serialization will make valid claims unverifiable or allow ambiguous payloads.
blockers.recommendation: Define one canonical byte format with test vectors shared by TS and Rust. Include golden fixtures for quiz HMAC, quiz_state_hash, ClaimMessage bytes, ed25519 signature, and Anchor verification.

blockers.concern: Claim-signer keypair compromise is unrecoverable because the Anchor program hardcodes the signer pubkey AND the upgrade authority is frozen post-deploy. A leaked CLAIM_SIGNER_SECRET enables forging ClaimMessage payloads to mint stones to arbitrary wallets indefinitely with no on-chain remediation path.
blockers.why_matters: Combines two one-way decisions (hardcoded signer + frozen upgrade authority) into a single unrecoverable failure mode. A single env-var leak — vercel access, accidental log emission, repo commit — becomes terminal. Even non-malicious key loss (former contributor leaves with access) bricks the system.
blockers.location: §5.2 (upgrade authority frozen), §5.1 (hardcoded claim-signer), §6.1 (CLAIM_SIGNER_SECRET stored in vercel env), D-13 (rotation deferred post-hackathon)
blockers.recommendation: Either (a) keep upgrade authority on a multisig until post-demo and only freeze AFTER an incident-free window, or (b) store claim-signer pubkey in a program-state account writable by a separate admin keypair so rotation is possible without redeploy. Document key-loss runbook BEFORE deploy. Treat 'frozen forever from minute one' as the actual highest-blast-radius decision in the SDD.

blockers.concern: Ed25519 instruction-sysvar verification is high risk and lacks precise constraints on instruction position, message offset parsing, duplicate ed25519 instructions, and canonical matching between instruction data and Anchor args.
blockers.why_matters: Incorrect parsing can let an attacker include a valid signature over different bytes, rely on a different ed25519 instruction in the transaction, or bypass intended signer/message binding.
blockers.recommendation: Use a known audited helper or write exhaustive tests for malformed offsets, wrong signer, wrong message, duplicate ed25519 instructions, reordered instructions, extra trailing bytes, and non-canonical ClaimMessage encodings. Require exact byte equality to the serialized Anchor argument.

blockers.concern: Ed25519-signature-via-instructions-sysvar verification reads `current_instruction_index - 1` and assumes that slot contains the Ed25519Program instruction. Solana transactions allow N instructions; nothing in the spec prevents an attacker from constructing a tx where the prior Ed25519Program instruction signs a DIFFERENT message that happens to validate, or where the program reads the wrong index because additional instructions are sandwiched.
blockers.why_matters: This is the load-bearing security primitive for the whole mint flow. The §5.1 pseudocode parses Ed25519 instruction data layout 'per Solana docs' but doesn't pin the offset-validation logic, doesn't explicitly check that ONLY one Ed25519Program instruction exists in the tx, and doesn't validate that the message bytes hash exactly matches `sha256(canonical_serialize(ClaimMessage args))`. Subtle bugs in this pattern have produced production exploits in other Solana programs.
blockers.recommendation: Spike 2 must produce a written threat-model checklist BEFORE building the real instruction: (1) reject if any Ed25519Program instruction is at an index other than expected, (2) canonical-serialize ClaimMessage with explicit field-tag prefixes (not just struct ordering), (3) byte-equal compare against the message in the Ed25519 instruction, (4) write fuzz tests against the parser. Treat passing spike-2 as 'pattern works' NOT 'invariants verified.'

blockers.concern: Freezing upgrade authority immediately post-deploy prevents critical bug fixes
blockers.why_matters: For a hackathon, freezing the contract immediately after deploy means any discovered bug (e.g., in signature verification or PDA logic) cannot be patched without deploying a entirely new program, breaking all client routing and requiring frontend updates.
blockers.recommendation: Retain upgrade authority during the evaluation phase, or transfer it to a multisig, instead of permanently freezing it on day 1.

blockers.concern: HMAC over quiz state uses 'length-prefixed CBOR' but no canonicalization specification. CBOR allows multiple valid encodings of the same logical value (integer width, map-key ordering, indefinite-length forms). Without a strict canonical-CBOR profile (e.g., RFC 8949 §4.2.1), two different byte sequences could decode to the same `(step, answers)` and produce different MACs, opening forgery, replay, and DoS surfaces.
blockers.why_matters: §3.2 explicitly forbids 'ambiguous concatenation' via length-prefix, but length-prefix alone is not canonicalization. An attacker who can submit step/answers via URL controls byte-level encoding and can probe for HMAC oracle behavior. Length-extension is golden-tested but encoding-malleability is not.
blockers.recommendation: Pin to RFC 8949 §4.2.1 canonical CBOR (or just use HMAC over a fixed-format string like `step|answer1|answer2|...` with explicit separator handling). Add an encoding-malleability fuzz test to the unit pyramid. State which CBOR library and which canonical-mode flag in the code.

blockers.concern: HMAC state does not bind to user wallet, allowing unlimited replay across different wallets
blockers.why_matters: A user can complete the quiz once to get a valid `(answers, mac)` pair, and submit it to the POST endpoint with thousands of different wallets. This bypasses the intended quiz effort and allows mass-minting Genesis Stones.
blockers.recommendation: Since GET cannot bind the wallet, the server must mark the `mac` or a unique `quiz_id` inside the HMAC payload as 'consumed' in Vercel KV upon a successful POST signature issuance.

blockers.concern: Nonce replay prevention is internally inconsistent: §3.3 says nonce is stored in Vercel KV, while §5.1 says the Anchor program rejects `nonce not in seen_nonces`. The on-chain program cannot consult Vercel KV, and an on-chain global seen-nonce set is not designed.
blockers.why_matters: Replay protection can fail or become impossible to implement safely. A valid signed ClaimMessage could be replayed until expiry unless replay state is enforced on-chain, but unbounded on-chain nonce storage can become a cost and scalability problem.
blockers.recommendation: Choose one authoritative replay model. Prefer an on-chain claim PDA keyed by wallet or claim hash for idempotence, plus expiry checks. If nonce uniqueness is required, define a bounded nonce PDA/account strategy and cleanup posture, or remove the claim that server-side KV prevents on-chain replay.

blockers.concern: Spike 1/2/3 'must pass by EOD 2026-05-08' but the SDD does not define (a) a specific time-of-day cutoff, (b) the go/no-go decision-maker, (c) what 'partial pass' means, or (d) how the deck-rewrite for Spike-1-fail (stretch #13, sprint-4 morning) reconciles with a failure detected on day 1. There is a 3-day gap between fail-detection and pre-staged fallback.
blockers.why_matters: With deadline 2026-05-11 and demo recording ~2026-05-10, a Spike-1 failure detected late on 2026-05-08 leaves the team committed to 'mint' language with no time to rewrite product framing. The brittleness lives in the gap between detection and decision. Stretch #13 places the deck-template work AFTER the time when the decision was supposed to bind.
blockers.recommendation: Move the 'pre-stage 2 deck templates' work to sprint-1 morning (parallel with spikes), not sprint-4. Define explicit Spike-1 cutoff (e.g., 18:00 PT 2026-05-08) and named decision-maker. Specify what counts as pass: NFT visible in Phantom collectibles tab on at least 2 wallets across 2 OS platforms within 30s of mint.

blockers.concern: Sponsored-payer wallet can be drained via intentional transaction failures
blockers.why_matters: The server partial-signs as `fee_payer`. An attacker can collect valid signed transactions and submit them repeatedly, or submit them to fail (e.g. hitting the idempotent PDA check). Solana charges the fee payer for failed transactions, allowing an attacker to drain the sponsored-payer funds very quickly.
blockers.recommendation: Shift the fee burden to the user (wallet), or introduce strong Sybil resistance (e.g., ReCaptcha) before the server issues a sponsored-payer signature.

blockers.concern: The Anchor program scope understates the complexity of minting a visible Metaplex NFT. A two-instruction program description omits mint account creation, token account handling, metadata PDA accounts, edition accounts, rent, authorities, compute budget, and CPI signer seeds.
blockers.why_matters: The day-1 or sprint-3 plan may discover that the program/account surface is much larger than designed, causing schedule failure or insecure shortcuts around metadata and mint authority.
blockers.recommendation: Expand the Anchor account model before implementation. List all accounts required for mint, metadata, master edition if used, token program, associated token program, system program, rent/sysvars, and CPI signer authority. Keep PDA-only fallback as the default until the Metaplex spike passes.

blockers.concern: The mint Action state handoff is underspecified and may be non-functional: the result button href is `/api/actions/mint/genesis-stone`, but the POST contract expects `{ account, answers, mac }`. Solana Action POST bodies are wallet-controlled and typically provide the account/signature context, not arbitrary quiz answers unless encoded in the action href or parameters.
blockers.why_matters: The core vertical slice can reach the archetype result and then fail at the only signing step because the server cannot reconstruct or validate the quiz state at mint time.
blockers.recommendation: Define the exact Action POST state transport. Put the validated quiz state or opaque claim token in the mint href query string, or issue a server-side claim session ID from `/result` with TTL and bind it during POST. Add an integration test using the actual Dialect/Actions inspector payload shape.

blockers.concern: The sponsored-payer demo override disables the halt condition while allowing unbounded mint attempts, and the stated conservation invariant is not tied to an enforcement mechanism.
blockers.why_matters: A bot or repeated failed flow during the demo window can drain the payer, break the live demo, or create uncontrolled spend. Logging every 30 seconds detects damage after it starts but does not prevent it.
blockers.recommendation: Keep a hard per-window mint cap even when `DISABLE_PAYER_HALT=true`. Enforce wallet/IP/session quotas before transaction creation, reserve estimated fees before signing, and fail closed when committed plus reserved mints exceeds the payer budget.

blockers.concern: Vercel KV nonce store is named as the durability fix for replay protection but the SDD does not specify provisioning steps, region, consistency model, or fallback behavior on KV outage. Vercel KV reads can be eventually consistent across regions; a fast-replay attack within the consistency window could double-spend a nonce.
blockers.why_matters: The replay-protection guarantee depends on read-after-write consistency for the nonce check. If KV is configured for read-replica reads (default in some tiers), nonce_seen=false can persist beyond the actual write. The §6.2 conservation invariant assumes nonce uniqueness; without it, sponsored-payer drain-via-replay becomes possible.
blockers.location: §3.3 (Vercel KV note), §4.2 step 5 (nonce generation), §6.1 (no KV in keypair model)
blockers.recommendation: Specify KV configuration: strongly-consistent reads, single-region (likely iad1), `SET nonce ... NX EX 300` atomic check-and-set (not GET-then-SET). Add KV provisioning to sprint-1 spine checklist. Document fallback when KV is unreachable: fail-closed (503) not fail-open. Alert on KV latency p99 > 100ms during demo.
